mn29:0.2: The Greater Simile of the Heartwood
mn29:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Rājagaha, on Vulture Peak Mountain, not long after Devadatta had gone away.
mn29:1.3: There the *Bhagavā*, concerning Devadatta, addressed the *bhikkhus*:
mn29:2.1: “Here, *bhikkhus*, a certain son of a good family, out of faith, has gone forth from the household life into homelessness:
mn29:2.10: A clear-sighted person, seeing him, would say:
mn29:2.11: ‘This man surely did not understand heartwood, did not understand sapwood, did not understand bark, did not understand outer bark, did not understand branches and leaves.
mn29:2.12: And yet this man, wanting heartwood, seeking heartwood, going about in search of heartwood, passed right by the heartwood, passed by the sapwood, passed by the bark, passed by the outer bark of a great standing tree full of heartwood, cut off its branches and leaves, took them, and departed thinking, “Heartwood.”
mn29:2.13: And he will not obtain the benefit to be obtained from heartwood.’
mn29:2.14: So too, *bhikkhus*, here a certain son of a good family, out of faith, has gone forth from the household life into homelessness:
mn29:2.15: ‘I am beset by birth, aging, and death, by sorrows, lamentations, *dukkha*, distress, and despair, overcome by *dukkha* [*dukkhotiṇṇo*], overwhelmed by *dukkha* [*dukkhapareto*].
mn29:2.16: Perhaps an ending of this whole mass of *dukkha* might be found.’
mn29:2.17: Having gone forth in this way, he acquires gains, honor, and praise.
mn29:2.18: He is pleased with those gains, honor, and praise, and thinks his aim fulfilled.
mn29:2.19: He exalts himself and disparages others because of those gains, honor, and praise: ‘I am one with gains, honor, and praise; but these other *bhikkhus* are obscure and insignificant.’
mn29:2.2: ‘I am beset by birth, aging, and death, by sorrows, lamentations, *dukkha*, distress, and despair, overcome by *dukkha* [*dukkhotiṇṇo*], overwhelmed by *dukkha* [*dukkhapareto*].
mn29:2.20: He becomes intoxicated by those gains, honor, and praise, becomes negligent, falls into negligence; and being negligent, he dwells in *dukkha*.
mn29:2.21: This is called, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* who has seized the branches and leaves of the holy life;
mn29:2.22: and with that he has come to a standstill.
mn29:2.3: Perhaps an ending of this whole mass of *dukkha* might be found.’
mn29:2.4: Having gone forth in this way, he acquires gains, honor, and praise.
mn29:2.5: He is pleased with those gains, honor, and praise, and thinks his aim fulfilled.
mn29:2.6: He exalts himself and disparages others because of those gains, honor, and praise:
mn29:2.7: ‘I am one with gains, honor, and praise; but these other *bhikkhus* are obscure and insignificant.’
mn29:2.8: He becomes intoxicated by those gains, honor, and praise, becomes negligent, falls into negligence; and being negligent, he dwells in *dukkha*.
mn29:2.9: Just as, *bhikkhus*, a man wanting heartwood, seeking heartwood, going about in search of heartwood, might pass right by the heartwood, pass by the sapwood, pass by the bark, pass by the outer bark of a great standing tree full of heartwood, cut off its branches and leaves, take them, and depart thinking, ‘Heartwood.’
mn29:3.1: But here, *bhikkhus*, a certain son of a good family, out of faith, has gone forth from the household life into homelessness:
mn29:3.10: He exalts himself and disparages others because of that accomplishment in ethical conduct:
mn29:3.11: ‘I am ethical, characterized by good *dhamma*s [*kalyāṇadhammo*]; but these other *bhikkhus* are unethical, characterized by bad *dhamma*s [*pāpadhammā*].’
mn29:3.12: He becomes intoxicated by that accomplishment in ethical conduct, becomes negligent, falls into negligence; and being negligent, he dwells in *dukkha*.
mn29:3.13: Just as, *bhikkhus*, a man wanting heartwood, seeking heartwood, going about in search of heartwood, might pass right by the heartwood, pass by the sapwood, pass by the bark of a great standing tree full of heartwood, cut off the outer bark, take it, and depart thinking, ‘Heartwood.’
mn29:3.14: A clear-sighted person, seeing him, would say:
mn29:3.15: ‘This man surely did not understand heartwood, did not understand sapwood, did not understand bark, did not understand outer bark, did not understand branches and leaves.
mn29:3.16: And yet this man, wanting heartwood, seeking heartwood, going about in search of heartwood, passed right by the heartwood, passed by the sapwood, passed by the bark of a great standing tree full of heartwood, cut off the outer bark, took it, and departed thinking, “Heartwood.”
mn29:3.17: And he will not obtain the benefit to be obtained from heartwood.’
mn29:3.2: ‘I am beset by birth, aging, and death, by sorrows, lamentations, *dukkha*, distress, and despair, overcome by *dukkha* [*dukkhotiṇṇo*], overwhelmed by *dukkha* [*dukkhapareto*].
mn29:3.3: Perhaps an ending of this whole mass of *dukkha* might be found.’
mn29:3.4: Having gone forth in this way, he acquires gains, honor, and praise.
mn29:3.5: He is not pleased with those gains, honor, and praise, and does not think his aim fulfilled.
mn29:3.6: He does not exalt himself or disparage others because of those gains, honor, and praise.
mn29:3.7: He does not become intoxicated by those gains, honor, and praise, does not become negligent, does not fall into negligence.
mn29:3.8: Being non-negligent, he achieves accomplishment in ethical conduct.
mn29:3.9: He is pleased with that accomplishment in ethical conduct, and thinks his aim fulfilled.
mn29:4.1: So too, *bhikkhus*, here a certain son of a good family, out of faith, has gone forth from the household life into homelessness:
mn29:4.10: He exalts himself and disparages others because of that accomplishment in ethical conduct:

mn29:4.14: and with that he has come to a standstill.

mn29:4.29: He becomes intoxicated by that accomplishment in *samādhi* [*samādhisampadāya*], becomes negligent, falls into negligence; and being negligent, he dwells in *dukkha*.
mn29:4.3: Perhaps an ending of this whole mass of *dukkha* might be found.’

mn29:5.14: He does not become intoxicated by that accomplishment in *samādhi* [*samādhisampadāya*], does not become negligent, does not fall into negligence. Being non-negligent, he achieves knowledge and vision.

mn29:5.18: But these other *bhikkhus* dwell not knowing and not seeing.’

mn29:5.39: He exalts himself and disparages others because of that knowledge and vision:
mn29:5.4: Having gone forth in this way, he acquires gains, honor, and praise.
mn29:5.40: ‘I dwell knowing and seeing, but these other *bhikkhus* dwell not knowing and not seeing.’
mn29:5.41: He becomes intoxicated by that knowledge and vision, becomes negligent, falls into negligence; and being negligent, he dwells in *dukkha*.
mn29:5.42: This is called, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* who has seized the sapwood of the holy life;
mn29:5.43: and with that he has come to a standstill.

mn29:6.1: But here, *bhikkhus*, a certain son of a good family, out of faith, has gone forth from the household life into homelessness:
mn29:6.10: He does not become intoxicated by that accomplishment in ethical conduct, does not become negligent, does not fall into negligence. Being non-negligent, he achieves accomplishment in *samādhi* [*samādhisampadaṁ*].
mn29:6.11: He is pleased with that accomplishment in *samādhi* [*samādhisampadāya*], but does not think his aim fulfilled.
mn29:6.12: He does not exalt himself or disparage others because of that accomplishment in *samādhi* [*samādhisampadāya*].
mn29:6.13: He does not become intoxicated by that accomplishment in *samādhi* [*samādhisampadāya*], does not become negligent, does not fall into negligence. Being non-negligent, he achieves knowledge and vision.
mn29:6.14: He is pleased with that knowledge and vision, but does not think his aim fulfilled.
mn29:6.15: He does not exalt himself or disparage others because of that knowledge and vision.
mn29:6.16: He does not become intoxicated by that knowledge and vision, does not become negligent, does not fall into negligence. Being non-negligent, he achieves non-temporary liberation.
mn29:6.17: This is impossible, *bhikkhus*, there is no chance of it, that that *bhikkhu* would fall away from that non-temporary liberation.
mn29:6.18: Just as, *bhikkhus*, a man wanting heartwood, seeking heartwood, going about in search of heartwood, might cut out just the heartwood from a great standing tree full of heartwood, take it, and depart knowing, ‘Heartwood.’
mn29:6.19: A clear-sighted person, seeing him, would say:
mn29:6.2: ‘I am beset by birth, aging, and death, by sorrows, lamentations, *dukkha*, distress, and despair, overcome by *dukkha* [*dukkhotiṇṇo*], overwhelmed by *dukkha* [*dukkhapareto*].
mn29:6.20: ‘This man has understood heartwood, has understood sapwood, has understood bark, has understood outer bark, has understood branches and leaves.
mn29:6.21: And indeed this man, wanting heartwood, seeking heartwood, going about in search of heartwood, cut out just the heartwood from a great standing tree full of heartwood, took it, and departed knowing, “Heartwood.”
mn29:6.22: And he will obtain the benefit to be obtained from heartwood.’
mn29:6.23: So too, *bhikkhus*, here a certain son of a good family, out of faith, has gone forth from the household life into homelessness:
mn29:6.24: ‘I am beset by birth, aging, and death, by sorrows, lamentations, *dukkha*, distress, and despair, overcome by *dukkha* [*dukkhotiṇṇo*], overwhelmed by *dukkha* [*dukkhapareto*].
mn29:6.25: Perhaps an ending of this whole mass of *dukkha* might be found.’
mn29:6.26: Having gone forth in this way, he acquires gains, honor, and praise.
mn29:6.27: He is not pleased with those gains, honor, and praise, and does not think his aim fulfilled.
mn29:6.28: He does not exalt himself or disparage others because of those gains, honor, and praise.
mn29:6.29: He does not become intoxicated by those gains, honor, and praise, does not become negligent, does not fall into negligence. Being non-negligent, he achieves accomplishment in ethical conduct.
mn29:6.3: Perhaps an ending of this whole mass of *dukkha* might be found.’
mn29:6.30: He is pleased with that accomplishment in ethical conduct, but does not think his aim fulfilled.
mn29:6.31: He does not exalt himself or disparage others because of that accomplishment in ethical conduct.
mn29:6.32: He does not become intoxicated by that accomplishment in ethical conduct, does not become negligent, does not fall into negligence. Being non-negligent, he achieves accomplishment in *samādhi* [*samādhisampadaṁ*].
mn29:6.33: He is pleased with that accomplishment in *samādhi* [*samādhisampadāya*], but does not think his aim fulfilled.
mn29:6.34: He does not exalt himself or disparage others because of that accomplishment in *samādhi* [*samādhisampadāya*].
mn29:6.35: He does not become intoxicated by that accomplishment in *samādhi* [*samādhisampadāya*], does not become negligent, does not fall into negligence. Being non-negligent, he achieves knowledge and vision.
mn29:6.36: He is pleased with that knowledge and vision, but does not think his aim fulfilled.
mn29:6.37: He does not exalt himself or disparage others because of that knowledge and vision.
mn29:6.38: He does not become intoxicated by that knowledge and vision, does not become negligent, does not fall into negligence. Being non-negligent, he achieves non-temporary liberation.
mn29:6.39: This is impossible, *bhikkhus*, there is no chance of it, that that *bhikkhu* would fall away from that non-temporary liberation.
mn29:6.4: Having gone forth in this way, he acquires gains, honor, and praise.
mn29:6.5: He is not pleased with those gains, honor, and praise, and does not think his aim fulfilled.
mn29:6.6: He does not exalt himself or disparage others because of those gains, honor, and praise.
mn29:6.7: He does not become intoxicated by those gains, honor, and praise, does not become negligent, does not fall into negligence. Being non-negligent, he achieves accomplishment in ethical conduct.
mn29:6.8: He is pleased with that accomplishment in ethical conduct, but does not think his aim fulfilled.
mn29:6.9: He does not exalt himself or disparage others because of that accomplishment in ethical conduct.
mn29:7.1: So, *bhikkhus*, this holy life is not for the benefit of gains, honor, and praise, nor for the benefit of accomplishment in ethical conduct, nor for the benefit of accomplishment in *samādhi* [*samādhisampadānisaṁsaṁ*], nor for the benefit of knowledge and vision.
mn29:7.2: And whatever is this unshakable liberation of mind—
mn29:7.3: it is for the sake of this, *bhikkhus*, that this holy life is lived; this is the heartwood, this is the culmination.”
mn29:7.5: Those *bhikkhus*, pleased, delighted in what was said by the *Bhagavā*.
mn29:7.6: The Greater Simile of the Heartwood is finished, the ninth.
